source_metadata.title_raw: Randy J. Risner - A-19-139 - August 22, 2019 - Vallejo

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 22, 2019

Randy J. Risner
Chief Assistant City Attorney
Vallejo City Attorney's Office
555 Santa Clara Street
Vallejo, CA 94590

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-139

Dear Mr. Risner:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Vallejo City Manager Greg
Nyhoff regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please
note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not
under any other conflict of interest laws. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering
advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are
complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should
change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit the City Manager from taking part in governmental decisions relating
to the City's potential reduction of Mello-Roos Community Facilities District ("CFD") assessments
on parcels located within the City's Mare Island CFD, and reevaluation of the use of Mare Island
CFD assessment funds, given that the City Manager leases his residence located within the Mare
Island CFD and that the parcel underlying his residence is subject to a Mare Island CFD
assessment?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit the City Manager from taking part in decisions relating to the
Mare Island CFD assessments because those decisions' effect on the City Manager's real property
interest in his leased residence would be indistinguishable from those decisions' effect on the public
generally pursuant to Regulation 18703(e)(1).

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-139
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are the Chief Assistant City Attorney for the City of Vallejo and the authorized
representative of Vallejo City Manager Greg Nyhoff. Located in the westernmost portion of the
City, Mare Island is a peninsula surrounded by the San Pablo Bay to the west, the Carquinez Strait
to the south, and the Napa River to the east. The Napa River bisects the City, and the rest of the
City is located west of the River. Mare Island was the longtime site of the former Mare Island Naval
Shipyard, which was decommissioned on April 1, 1996 as part of the Base Realignment and
Closure process of 1993. The real property underlying the former Shipyard was transferred to the
City, and a portion of the property was then sold and developed into neighborhoods of single-family
residences.

The Mello-Roos Community Facilities Act of 1982 (Section 53311 et seq.) authorizes a
local government agency to form a "Community Facilities District" ("CFD") to finance public
improvements and services within the CFD. Local government agencies normally form a CFD in an
undeveloped area to fund the public improvements and services necessary for residential or
commercial development within that area. Once a CFD is formed, the local government agency
levies a "CFD assessment" against each real property parcel within the CFD, and the owner of the
parcel is obligated to pay the CFD assessment each year. Over time, the CFD assessments pay off
the bonded debt taken on to fund the CFD's public improvements and services. Each CFD
assessment is based on the application of a mathematical formula that considers the parcel's
characteristics, such as its use, lot size, or the square footage of structures located thereon.

The City has created several CFDs within its boundaries, and levied CFD assessments on
the real property parcels within those Districts. The City Manager leases a single-family residence
located on Mare Island on a biannual basis, that residence is within the City's Mare Island CFD,
and the Mare Island CFD's assessments are significantly higher than the City's other CFDs'
assessments. Owners of parcels within the Mare Island CFD have recently requested the reduction
of their CFD assessments because they are significantly higher than the CFD assessments in the
City's other CFDs. Those parcel owners have also requested that the City reevaluate how their CFD
assessment funds are used within the Mare Island CFD.

You state that: neither the potential reduction of Mare Island CFD assessments, nor the
potential reevaluation of the use of the funds derived from those assessments, is likely to result in
the discontinuation of any major public improvements or services within the Mare Island CFD;
pending development within the Mare Island CFD is likely to mitigate any loss of funding resultant
from the City's potential reduction of the Mare Island CFD assessments; and the City is only in the
preliminary stages of considering these issues, and it is possible that no changes will be made to the
Mare Island CFD assessments. On August 13, 2019, you confirmed that the public improvements
and services funded by the Mare Island CFD assessments are broadly provided within that CFD,
and that the Mare Island CFD assessments are proportionally applied to each real property parcel
within the Mare Island CFD according to a mathematical formula.

File No. A-19-139
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from those
decisions' effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that
may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are identified in Section 87103.
With respect to decisions relating to the Mare Island CFD assessments at issue, the City Manager
has the following interests, based on the facts presented:

• A real property interest in his leased residence because he has a leasehold interest in that real
property worth $2,000 or more. (See Section 87103(b).)

• An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family
members. (See Section 87103.)²

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in
the decision; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the interest. A real property interest is also explicitly involved in a governmental
decision if the decision affects the interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). Under
Regulation 18702.2(a)(3) an interest in real property is explicitly involved in any decision that
would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the parcel.
Accordingly, the City Manager's real property interest in his leased residence is explicitly involved
in decisions relating to the Mare Island CFD assessments at issue.

Regulation 18702.2 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on
an official's real property interest, and subdivision (c) of that regulation provides that the decision's
effect on an official's real property interest in a leasehold is material only if the decision will: (1)
change the termination date of the lease; (2) increase or decrease the potential rental value of the
property; (3) change the official's actual or legally allowable use of the property; or (4) impact the
official's use and enjoyment of the property.

If the City reduces the Mare Island CFD assessment applicable to the City Manager's
residence which he leases on a biannual basis, the owner of that parcel may decrease the rent he or
she charges to lease that residence barring other indications that it is unlikely that the reduced
assessment would result in a rent reduction such as a minimal change in the assessment or an
uncompetitive rental market. However, it is unnecessary in the present circumstances to determine
whether the assessment decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on

² Under Regulation 18702.5(c), if the governmental decision affects a business entity or real property in which
the official has an interest, an effect on the official's personal finances is not considered separately and materiality is
determined under Regulation 18702.1 or 18702.2. Accordingly, we do not further analyze any effect on the City
Manager's personal finances.

File No. A-19-139
Page No. 4

the City Manager's real property interest in his leased residence in light of the analysis of the decisions' effect on the public generally below.

Public Generally Exception – Public Services and Utilities

Even assuming a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on an official's interest, Regulation 18703 sets forth the various iterations of the Public Generally Exception to the Act's conflict of interest provisions, and subdivision (e)(1) of that regulation provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest is deemed indistinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally if the decision establishes or adjusts an assessment for water, utilities, or other broadly provided public services or facilities that are applied equally, proportionally, or by the same percentage to the official's interest and other properties subject to the assessment.

Based upon the facts provided, the Mare Island CFD assessments fund public improvements and services that are broadly provided within the Mare Island CFD, and those assessments are proportionally applied to each real property parcel within the Mare Island CFD according to a mathematical formula. Therefore, the Public Generally Exception for Public Services and Utilities set forth in Regulation 18703(e)(1) applies. Thus, the Act does not prohibit the City Manager from taking part in decisions relating to the Mare Island CFD assessments because those decisions' effect on the City Manager's real property interest in his leased residence would be indistinguishable from their effect on the public generally pursuant to Regulation 18703(e)(1).

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

BGL:aja

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit the City Manager from taking part in governmental decisions relating 
to the City's potential reduction of Mello-Roos Community Facilities District ("CFD") assessments 
on parcels located within the City's Mare Island CFD, and reevaluation of the use of Mare Island 
CFD assessment funds, given that the City Manager leases his residence located within the Mare 
Island CFD and that the parcel underlying his residence is subject to a Mare Island CFD 
assessment?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the City Manager from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Mare Island CFD assessments because those decisions' effect on the City Manager's real property 
interest in his leased residence would be indistinguishable from those decisions' effect on the public 
generally pursuant to Regulation 18703(e)(l).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit the City Manager from taking part in governmental decisions relating 
to the City's potential reduction of Mello-Roos Community Facilities District ("CFD") assessments 
on parcels located within the City's Mare Island CFD, and reevaluation of the use of Mare Island 
CFD assessment funds, given that the City Manager leases his residence located within the Mare 
Island CFD and that the parcel underlying his residence is subject to a Mare Island CFD 
assessment?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the City Manager from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Mare Island CFD assessments because those decisions' effect on the City Manager's real property 
interest in his leased residence would be indistinguishable from those decisions' effect on the public 
generally pursuant to Regulation 18703(e)(l).
